status_notes: Initial research from Google Cloud IAM documentation, 2026-04-04. Pass 1 (service docs). Needs Pass 2 (API), Pass 3 (cross-reference), and Pass 5 (human review with live GCP environment).
official_name: Google Cloud Identity and Access Management
notes: Foundation identity and access management for Google Cloud. Provides role-based access control, IAM conditions (ABAC), deny policies, principal access boundary policies, service accounts, workload identity federation, organization policies, and audit logging. IAM policies (allow, deny, PAB) work together to determine effective access.

match_nodes.description: Standard IAM allow policies granting access to resources

data.description: JSON/YAML policies attached to resources that bind principals (users, groups, service accounts, domains) to roles. Primary mechanism for granting access in Google Cloud. Policies are inherited down the resource hierarchy (organization → folder → project → resource). Each binding associates a set of principals with a role and optional conditions.

options.description: Who: user, serviceAccount, group, domain, allUsers, allAuthenticatedUsers
What permissions: basic, predefined, or custom role
When: optional CEL expression restricting when the binding applies
Where: the resource the policy is attached to (project, folder, org, or specific resource)

data.vendor_recommendation: Use predefined roles for least privilege. Apply policies at the narrowest scope possible. Use conditions to further restrict access. Audit inherited policies via Policy Analyzer.

match_nodes.description: Explicit deny rules overriding allow policies

data.description: Policies that prevent principals from using specific permissions, regardless of allow policies. Evaluated before allow policies — deny always wins. Useful for setting hard guardrails that cannot be overridden by allow policy changes. Supports exception principals (identities exempted from the deny rule). Attached to organizations, folders, or projects.

options.description: Who is denied: principals the deny rule applies to
Who is exempted: principals excluded from this deny rule
What is denied: specific permissions to deny
When: optional CEL condition for conditional deny

data.vendor_recommendation: Use deny policies for hard security guardrails that should not be overridden. Examples: prevent external sharing, block certain API calls, restrict access to sensitive projects.

match_nodes.description: Maximum resource access limits for principals

data.description: Policies defining which resources a principal is eligible to access. Sets an upper bound on access, separate from role grants. Even if an allow policy grants a role, the principal cannot access resources outside their principal access boundary. Useful for ensuring principals can only access resources within specific projects or folders.
data.vendor_recommendation: Use principal access boundary policies to limit the blast radius of overly broad role grants. Apply to service accounts and external identities to ensure they cannot access resources outside their intended scope.

match_nodes.description: Predefined, basic, and custom roles for access control

data.description: Role-based access control with three tiers. Basic roles (Owner, Editor, Viewer) provide broad access across all services — legacy, not recommended for production. Predefined roles (1000+) are managed by Google and provide service-specific least-privilege access. Custom roles allow selecting specific permissions for organization-specific needs.

options.description: Broad access across all services. Owner includes Editor plus IAM management and billing. Legacy — avoid in production.
Google-managed, service-specific roles. Updated by Google as services evolve. Examples: roles/compute.admin, roles/storage.objectViewer, roles/bigquery.dataEditor.
User-defined roles selecting specific permissions. Created at org or project level. Max 300 per org, 300 per project.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use predefined roles for least privilege. Avoid basic roles (Owner/Editor/Viewer) in production — they grant far more access than needed. Create custom roles only when predefined roles are too broad. Use IAM Recommender to right-size roles based on actual usage.

match_nodes.description: Attribute-based access control using CEL expressions

data.description: Conditional role bindings using Common Expression Language (CEL). Restrict when an allow policy binding applies based on attributes of the request and resource. Supports: resource attributes (type, name, service, tags), request attributes (time, IP/access level via Access Context Manager), and principal attributes (tags). Conditions are evaluated at request time.

options.description: Resource type (e.g., storage.googleapis.com/Bucket)
Resource name, supports startsWith/endsWith for prefix matching
Google Cloud service (e.g., compute.googleapis.com)
Resource tag key-value pairs
Timestamp of the request, for time-based access
URL path and host, for API-level restrictions
Access Context Manager access levels (IP ranges, device policy)

data.vendor_recommendation: Use conditions to restrict time-based access (temporary grants), resource-based access (specific buckets or instances), and tag-based access (ABAC). Test conditions in report-only mode first.

match_nodes.description: Machine identities for workloads and automation

data.description: Non-human identities for applications and services. Can be attached to GCE instances, GKE pods, Cloud Functions, Cloud Run, App Engine, Dataflow, etc. Support key-based authentication (JSON/P12 key files — discouraged) and keyless authentication (attached service accounts, workload identity, impersonation). Each project can have up to 100 service accounts.

options.description: Service account attached to a compute resource (GCE, GKE, Cloud Functions). No keys needed — metadata server provides tokens.
Downloaded JSON key file for authentication outside GCP. Long-term credential — security risk if leaked.
PKCS12 key format. Legacy — prefer JSON or keyless alternatives.
One identity temporarily acts as a service account via generateAccessToken. Auditable, no key management.
Generate short-lived tokens (access tokens, ID tokens, JWTs) via IAM Credentials API.

data.vendor_recommendation: Avoid service account keys — they are long-term credentials that pose significant risk if leaked. Use attached service accounts for workloads on GCP. Use workload identity federation for external workloads. Use impersonation for admin/CI tasks. Disable unused service accounts before deleting (30-day undelete window).

match_nodes.description: External workload authentication without service account keys

data.description: Authenticate external workloads (AWS, Azure, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Kubernetes, OIDC providers, SAML providers) without service account keys. Uses workload identity pools and providers to establish trust with external identity providers. External workload presents a token from its provider, which is exchanged for a short-lived GCP access token.

options.description: AWS EC2/ECS/Lambda workloads authenticate using AWS STS credentials
Azure workloads authenticate using managed identity tokens
GitHub Actions workflows authenticate using OIDC tokens
GitLab CI jobs authenticate using OIDC tokens
External Kubernetes workloads authenticate using service account tokens
Any OIDC-compliant identity provider
SAML identity providers

data.vendor_recommendation: Use workload identity federation instead of service account keys for all external workloads. Configure attribute mappings and conditions to restrict which external identities can authenticate.

match_nodes.description: Organization-level constraints on Google Cloud resources

data.description: Constraints on Google Cloud resources at organization, folder, or project level. Controls resource configuration and behavior across the resource hierarchy. Examples: restrict service account key creation, limit VM external IPs, enforce uniform bucket-level access, restrict resource locations, disable service account creation, block external sharing.

options.description: Prevent creation of service account keys
Prevent creation of new service accounts
Restrict IAM policy members to specific domains
Restrict which VMs can have external IP addresses
Restrict where resources can be created (regions/zones)
Require uniform bucket-level access (disable object ACLs)
Require Shielded VM features on compute instances

data.vendor_recommendation: Use organization policies to enforce security baselines. Disable service account key creation where possible. Restrict resource locations to approved regions. Limit IAM policy members to your organization's domains.

match_nodes.description: IAM activity audit trail via Cloud Audit Logs

data.description: Cloud Audit Logs for IAM and all Google Cloud operations. Three log types: Admin Activity (always on, cannot be disabled, free — records IAM policy changes, resource creation/deletion), Data Access (configurable, chargeable — records resource reads/writes), and System Event (always on, free — records Google-initiated system actions). Integrates with Cloud Logging, BigQuery, Pub/Sub, and SIEM tools.

options.description: IAM policy changes, resource modifications. Always enabled, cannot be disabled, no charge.
Resource reads (ADMIN_READ, DATA_READ) and writes (DATA_WRITE). Configurable per service.
Google-initiated system operations. Always enabled, no charge.

default.value: Admin Activity and System Event logs are always enabled and cannot be disabled.
default.note: Data Access logs must be explicitly enabled per service and can generate high volume.

data.vendor_recommendation: Enable Data Access logs for security-sensitive services (IAM, Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Compute Engine). Export audit logs to a centralized logging project or SIEM. Set up log-based alerts for critical IAM changes.

configure.note: Data Access audit logs are configured via the auditConfigs section of the project IAM policy

match_nodes.description: Just-in-time temporary elevated access with approval workflows

data.description: Time-bound, approval-based temporary elevated access. Provides just-in-time privileged access with: entitlements (pre-defined role grants that can be requested), approval workflows (designated approvers), justification requirements, automatic revocation after time limit, and full audit trail. Available for project, folder, and organization-level roles.

options.description: Pre-configured eligible role grant with max duration and approval settings
Maximum time a grant can be active
Whether activation requires approval from designated approvers
Whether requester must provide a justification

data.vendor_recommendation: Use Privileged Access Manager for all elevated access grants. Define entitlements for commonly needed privileged roles. Require approval for high-impact roles. Set short maximum durations.
